The vision begins at the deepest point of Israel's loss, in order to show what God has already designed to replace what was destroyed.
40.2: God carries the prophet to Israel in vision and sets him on a towering mountain — the echo of Sinai, where Moses received the tabernacle pattern. The city-like structure to the south is the restored temple complex, shown to Ezekiel before it exists, as God once showed Moses the heavenly blueprint.
40.3: In the gateway stood a man blazing like polished bronze — an angelic architect carrying a linen cord for long distances and a reed for gate-by-gate precision. His appearance recalls the divine messenger of Ezekiel 1 and anticipates the one in Daniel 10. God's house will be measured to exact, holy specification.
40.4: 'Son of man, look, listen, concentrate' — the commission echoes Ezekiel 1 and 2 but with a crucial difference: here the vision is not a theophany of terror but a blueprint of hope. Ezekiel's task is to carry the architectural plans of the future temple back to a people who have no temple. The detailed measurements are the message: while Israel sits in Babylon, God is already designing what comes next.
40.5: The first measurement is the boundary wall — one reed thick, one reed high (about three meters each way on the long-cubit scale). The massive perimeter wall is the first thing measured because separation is the first principle of this temple: the wall announces what the entire vision is about — the boundary between the holy and the common world.
40.6: The vision begins with the east gate — the gate through which God's glory had departed in Ezekiel 10–11, and through which it will return in chapter 43. The measuring of the east gate is therefore the first movement of the restoration: the way is being prepared for the return of the King.
40.7: Six identical guardrooms — three per side — each a perfect reed-square, flanking the gate passage. Five-cubit gaps between each room allowed movement and passage. The gate is not merely an entrance but a structured corridor through guarded space: one does not stumble carelessly into God's courts.
40.8: Even the inner vestibule — the transitional threshold between the gate passage and the court — receives its precise measurement. In God's house, every threshold matters; no boundary is left undefined.
40.9: Eight cubits for the entry porch — a wide, authoritative threshold — flanked by two-cubit doorposts. The porch opens inward, drawing the worshiper deeper in. The gate architecture is designed not to keep people out but to govern the manner of entry.
40.10: Six identical guardrooms, perfectly bilateral. The symmetry is a theological statement: God's sanctuary is ordered, not improvised. The equal flanking architecture declares that no side of God's court is an afterthought.
40.11: Ten cubits wide — ample for the processional movement of worshipers and offerings. Thirteen cubits long — the full depth of the gateway. These are not cramped passages but generous entryways, sized for the dignity of approaching the King.
40.12: A one-cubit barrier before each guardroom kept the gateway floor clear for movement. Each room stood six cubits — one full measuring-reed — square. The consistent unit of measurement ties every element of the vision into a single, unified system of sacred proportion.
40.13: Twenty-five cubits — the internal width at the gate's broadest span. The phrase 'door opposite door' confirms the bilateral symmetry: the guardrooms face each other across the open passage, creating the ordered corridor that every entrant must traverse.
40.14: Sixty-cubit doorposts — architectural columns of commanding height — framed the gate against the surrounding court. Their grandeur signaled that the worshiper was entering a space of royal and divine significance.
40.15: Fifty cubits — the full depth of the gateway complex from its public threshold to its inner vestibule. The worshiper traversed fifty cubits before reaching the court itself: a measured journey, not a sudden arrival. The approach to God is always a traversal of defined, sacred distance.
40.16: Recessed windows — narrow on the outside, wider within — filled the gate interior with light without exposing the sacred space. Palm trees carved on every doorpost announced the recurring Eden motif: to enter this gate is to approach the garden of God's presence. The tree of life stands at every threshold.
40.17: The outer court: a paved expanse ringed by thirty chambers — ten per side — where Israel gathered. This is the place of the assembly, the zone open to the whole people. The pavement is the common ground; the chambers serve the needs of pilgrims, Levites, and worship functions. Everyone may stand here, though not all may advance.
40.18: The 'lower pavement' — so named because the inner court is elevated — runs parallel to the gate structures. The graduated elevation of the courts encodes a spatial theology: outer/lower, inner/higher, most holy/highest. Approach to God is also an ascent.
40.19: One hundred cubits — the span of the outer court on each measured side. This hundred-cubit interval between outer and inner courts is the architectural expression of graded holiness: the outer court welcomes all, but the inner court admits only those who draw nearer to God's presence.
40.20: The measuring angel now moves systematically to the north outer gate. The comprehensive survey of all gates and courts communicates that the entire perimeter of God's house is equally ordered: no gate is neglected, no approach is left approximate.
40.21: The north gate is identical to the east gate in every proportion. Uniformity is the point: no gate of God's house is more or less significant than another. Access is equally ordered from every direction.
40.22: Seven steps — the number of completeness — ascending to every outer gate. The approach is liturgical: seven steps is a movement through fullness before arrival. Palm trees at the vestibule mark the threshold as a garden passage. The identical motifs of every gate signal that there is one God whose house is approached from everywhere with the same honor.
40.23: Inner gates aligned with outer gates — axial corridors ran from each outer gate through the outer court to the corresponding inner gate. These sightlines directed the worshiper: you could look from the street straight through to the inner sanctuary. The approach to God has both direction and destination.
40.24: South, north, east — all three outer gates receive equal treatment. God's temple is not monumental on one side and modest on another; every direction of approach is equally honored.
40.25: The same recessed windows, the same proportions. Repetition in this vision is not monotony — it is the architectural statement that divine order is consistent, thorough, and without exception.
40.26: Palm trees on the doorposts of every gate — Eden's signature at every threshold. The seven ascending steps invite the worshiper into a rhythmic, measured approach: each step is a movement upward toward the holy.
40.27: The three outer-to-inner alignments complete the symmetrical layout: east, north, and south each hold a hundred-cubit span between outer and inner gates. The geometry of the court embodies divine order: measurable, consistent, complete.
40.28: Passing through the south gate into the inner court, the prophet now stands in the elevated, more restricted zone of holiness. The same beautiful proportions govern this gate as governed the outer gates — but the space it opens onto is reserved, set apart, one step closer to the presence of God.
40.29: The inner gates replicate the outer gates in every detail. Continuity of form at a deeper level of holiness: the same pattern, the same proportions, the same palm trees and windows — but now within the inner court.
40.30: A consistent portico system flanked every gate — twenty-five by five cubits, creating a sheltered transitional space at every threshold. In God's house, every crossing point is ceremonially marked.
40.31: The inner gates face outward — their vestibules look back toward the outer court, welcoming the ascending worshiper. Eight steps (one beyond the outer gates' seven) mark the inner gates as a higher ascent: the approach to the inner court costs an additional step of devotion.
40.32: The east inner gate — the culminating threshold on the axis that leads directly to the temple building. The measuring angel is drawing in toward the center: outer east gate, outer court, inner east gate, inner court, temple porch.
40.33: The repetition of measurements throughout this chapter is itself a theological act: God's order is consistent through every level of the approach. The text's insistence on identical proportions is not poor editing — it is the architectural liturgy of a God who does all things in proportion and completeness.
40.34: Palm trees at every inner gate as at every outer gate — the Eden imagery is relentless. The garden of God surrounds every threshold. Eight steps at every inner gate: the full ascent, one step beyond the outer gates, marking the advance into holier territory.
40.35: The north inner gate — the sixth and final gate of the systematic survey. The angel has now measured every gate: three outer, three inner, all identical. The comprehensive survey declares that no gate is afterthought, no entry is accidental.
40.36: The last gate's measurements complete what has been true throughout: perfect regularity. The vision is almost mathematical in its insistence — and that precision is itself the revelation. God's house is not improvised; it is designed.
40.37: Eight steps, palm trees, outward-facing vestibule — every inner gate ends with the same signature. The motifs form a unified language: Eden imagery (palm trees), ordered ascent (eight steps), welcoming orientation (facing the outer court). The six gates speak with one voice.
40.38: Practical holiness: beside the inner gate, a preparation chamber for washing the burnt offering before it reached the altar. The temple vision provides not only grand architectural visions but the mundane infrastructure of daily worship — because holiness extends to every act of preparation, not only to the culminating moment of sacrifice.
40.39: Four tables in the gate vestibule — the burnt offering (total consecration to God), the sin offering (atonement for unintentional transgression), the guilt offering (reparation for breach of covenant). All three major sacrifice types are prepared here, at the threshold between outer and inner court. The entire sacrificial system is concentrated at this gateway.
40.40: The preparation tables extend even to the exterior side of the inner gate vestibule — two more on each side, accessible from the outer court. The architecture of worship is thoroughly provisioned: no part of the sacrificial process is left without its assigned place.
40.41: Eight tables for slaughter — a bilateral, complete arrangement. Eight: one beyond the fullness of seven, the number of new beginning. The new temple's worship begins with thorough provision for sacrifice, because atonement is the foundation on which all approach to God rests.
40.42: Hewn stone — not wood, which absorbs blood, but durable, washable stone — for the sacrifice tables. One cubit high: working height, ergonomically designed for the priest to stand and prepare. One and a half cubits each way: proportioned for the work. Even the tools of slaughter had their assigned resting place on these tables. Holiness is not vague; it governs the details.
40.43: Hooks on the interior walls held carcasses for butchering; the tables received the cut portions for presentation. The vision provides for the full ritual workflow from slaughter to offering — every functional detail assigned its place, every act of preparation structured within the sacred order.
40.44: The singers — Levitical musicians — have their own chambers built into the inner court architecture, positioned to face each other across the open space. Song is not peripheral to this temple; it is built in. The restored worship of God includes music, art, and voice as structural elements, not ornaments.
40.45: The angel interprets the architecture: the south-facing priests' chamber (watching the approach from the south) belongs to those who guard and maintain the sanctuary itself. Priestly ministry includes not only sacrifice but the daily work of keeping the house of God in order — custodianship as a sacred vocation.
40.46: 'The sons of Zadok' — the priestly family singled out in Ezekiel for their faithfulness when the rest of Israel's priests led the nation into idolatry (44:15). Their reward is built into the very floor plan: the altar-keepers, those who stand closest to Yahweh, are the ones who remained loyal when loyalty was costly. The temple vision rewards faithfulness with nearness.
40.47: The inner court is a perfect square: a hundred by a hundred cubits — the number of fullness and completeness in a bounded space. The altar stands 'before the house,' on the axis between the inner gate and the temple entrance. This is where heaven and earth meet in sacrifice: the geographic and theological center of the entire vision.
40.48: The prophet now stands at the threshold of the temple building itself — the outer face of God's house. Five-cubit doorposts flanking a three-cubit-wide passage: narrower than the great outer gates, more intimate, more demanding. The nearer one approaches the presence of God, the more precisely one must orient oneself to enter.
40.49: Twenty by eleven cubits — a wide, welcoming approach before the narrow temple door. The flanking columns echo Solomon's famous pillars Jachin and Boaz (1 Kgs 7:21), tying this restored temple to the one it replaces. Steps leading up to the vestibule complete the layered ascent: outer court at ground level, inner court elevated, temple porch elevated further. The worshiper has been climbing the whole time.

41.1: The nave — the Holy Place — is entered through six-cubit doorposts on each side. The word 'tabernacle' (אֹהֶל, tent) echoes here: this permanent stone structure carries the theological heritage of the portable tent where God first dwelt with Israel in the wilderness. The new temple inherits the old sanctuary's meaning.
41.2: Forty cubits long by twenty cubits wide — the nave of the Holy Place. The number forty recurs throughout Israel's sacred history: forty years in the wilderness, forty-day fasts, forty-year reigns. The Holy Place is a room proportioned to the weight of that history. Ten-cubit entrance: wide enough for priests carrying holy implements but not a casual doorway.
41.3: The measuring angel enters the Most Holy Place alone — Ezekiel waits at the nave threshold, for only the high priest could enter the innermost room, once a year. Seven cubits: the breadth of the holiest threshold carries the number of divine completeness. The entrance to the Most Holy Place is seven cubits wide.
41.4: 'This is the Most Holy Place' — a perfect cube: twenty by twenty cubits. As Solomon's Holy of Holies was a perfect cube of twenty cubits (1 Kgs 6:20), this room maintains that sacred geometry. A cube in Scripture is the shape of completeness: in Revelation 21, the New Jerusalem is also a cube. The Most Holy Place is the architectural center of the cosmos where God's presence dwells.
41.5: Six-cubit walls for the temple building — massive, permanent, secure. The four-cubit side chambers built around the exterior of the walls provided structural buttressing and storage. The temple is not a thin-walled pavilion but a fortified treasury of the holy.
41.6: Ninety side chambers in three tiers, thirty per story — an enormous storage and service infrastructure surrounding the temple. Built into the wall offsets rather than the wall itself, they were structurally attached but not structurally dependent: the temple's integrity was independent of its annexed chambers, just as the holiness of God's presence is not dependent on the structures that serve it.
41.7: The tiered side chambers widened at each level — an architectural inversion of what one might expect. The temple building broadened upward, and the chambers grew with it. The winding stairway spiraling upward through the three stories carried worshipers and priests through a literally ascending journey: everything in this temple moves upward toward the holy.
41.8: The platform on which the entire temple stood was elevated one full reed above the surrounding terrain. The house of God sat literally higher than the world around it. Physical elevation expressed spiritual elevation: the temple was the highest point — architecturally and theologically — in restored Israel.
41.9: Twenty cubits of open space surrounded the temple on all sides between the side chambers and the outer courts — a buffer zone of light and air that separated the temple proper from everything else. Even the space around the Most Holy was ordered, measured, and maintained as sacred distance.
41.10: The twenty-cubit open space recurs on every side — a consistent moat of separation around the temple. No structure pressed against the walls of the house of God; it stood alone in its own measured space.
41.11: The side chambers opened not toward the temple wall but toward the open space — their doors faced north and south, giving access to the lateral service corridors. Five cubits: a narrow but navigable passage, sufficient for priestly movement and supply.
41.12: The large western building — facing the 'separate yard' (the buffer zone between temple and outer wall) — was ninety by seventy cubits with five-cubit walls. Its function is not identified here; it lies behind the temple, west of the Most Holy Place, in the most restricted zone of the complex. Its scale suggests a significant but unnamed sacred purpose.
41.13: The temple and the adjacent western complex both span a hundred cubits — the east-west axis of the entire sacred precinct measures in hundreds. The hundred-cubit module structures the entire temple plan, just as the inner court measured a hundred by a hundred cubits.
41.14: East, west, north, south — in every direction, the dimensions of the temple complex resolve into consistent hundreds. The architectural plan is not accidental but deliberately proportioned to a hundred-cubit module, declaring the completeness and finality of God's design.
41.15: A final hundred-cubit confirmation: the building behind the temple, including its flanking galleries, matched the master module. The inner nave and court vestibules are named here in a summary reference — everything has been measured, everything fits.
41.16: The interior surfaces of the temple were entirely wood-paneled — thresholds, windows, all three levels of the galleries. Wood covered the stone walls, creating warmth and resonance inside the house of God. The windows were covered (perhaps with lattice or screen), filtering light into the interior with controlled dignity.
41.17: Every surface — above every door, the inner sanctuary walls, all exterior faces — received paneling, and every panel was measured. 'By measure' is the refrain of this vision: nothing in God's house is eyeballed or approximated. Precision in materials, precision in dimensions, precision in finish.
41.18: Cherubim alternating with palm trees covered every paneled wall — a single image of Eden's guardians and Eden's garden woven together. Every surface declared the same thing: you are standing in the presence of the Holy One, in the garden of God, guarded by his heavenly attendants.
41.19: Two-faced cherubim — human and lion. Not the fearsome four-faced creatures of Ezekiel 1 and 10, but a domesticated, decorative pair: wisdom (human) and authority (lion). The temple's carvings distill the cherubim to their two most human-facing aspects, appropriate for the space where God dwells with his redeemed people.
41.20: Floor to ceiling, Eden motifs. No inch of the wall left plain. The worshiper entering the Holy Place was surrounded on all sides by the imagery of the garden and its guardians — standing, in a sense, inside the very boundary of paradise that human sin had closed.
41.21: Squared doorposts — the precise, clean lines of a perfectly crafted threshold. The symmetry between nave and sanctuary confirmed that the two inner rooms shared a unified aesthetic: one architectural language spoke from nave to Most Holy Place, from the outer approach to the innermost chamber.
41.22: The wooden altar in the Holy Place — called here 'the table before the LORD,' combining the incense altar and the table of showbread. Wood, not stone or bronze: the table of intimate presentation, not the altar of consuming fire. This table stands before the divine presence in the innermost accessible room. 'Before the LORD' — the phrase used for the table of bread in the tabernacle (Ex 25:30). The continual offering of bread and incense is maintained in the restored temple.
41.23: Double doors for both the nave and the Most Holy Place — pairs of doors, as in Solomon's temple (1 Kgs 6:31–35), adding solemnity to each threshold. A single door can be pushed open carelessly; double doors require intention.
41.24: Four panels in total, two for each doorway — each panel turning on its own hinge, each requiring its own deliberate opening. The layered, multiplied thresholds of the temple make every entry a conscious act, every passage an intentional crossing.
41.25: Cherubim and palm trees followed the worshiper even to the doors — the Eden motif carved into every surface that divides sacred space. The wooden canopy over the outer vestibule provided both shelter and architectural dignity: entering the temple was sheltered passage, not exposure.
41.26: Palm trees and recessed windows everywhere — down to the canopy beams and sidewalls of the vestibule. The Eden imagery was not reserved for the holiest rooms; it extended to every surface that framed the approach. God's house is a unified whole: from the perimeter wall to the canopy beam, every element proclaimed the same sacred reality.

42.1: The measuring angel now turns to the northern priests' chambers — the functional infrastructure of the temple complex. Chapter 42 closes the architectural survey by showing where the priests will live and work: adjacent to the separate yard, in chambers that maintain both practicality and holiness.
42.2: A hundred-cubit length by fifty-cubit breadth — the chamber complex matches the hundred-cubit module that governs the entire temple plan. Even the priests' living quarters participate in the sacred geometry.
42.3: Three stories of galleries ran between the inner court's twenty-cubit buffer and the outer court's pavement — tiered balconies creating a visual and spatial bridge between the two zones of the court. The priests who served in both courts could inhabit the space between them.
42.4: A ten-cubit-wide internal corridor ran the full length of the chamber building — a hundred-cubit passage giving access to every room along the north. The doors opened north, away from the sanctuary proper, maintaining the spatial logic that servants do not stand in the way of the One they serve.
42.5: A structural necessity: the projecting galleries reduced the floor area of the upper story. The tiered complexity of the chambers reflects both practical engineering and a kind of organic hierarchy — even the priests' quarters have a spatial order, lower and upper, practical and symbolic.
42.6: The chambers had no court pillars to bear their weight — they rose by their own walls, without external columns. The lack of open colonnades meant each story had to be stepped back to distribute weight, creating the tiered profile. Even without the grand colonnade of the outer court, the priests' chambers were solidly built — sufficient, purposeful, unceremonious.
42.7: A fifty-cubit outer wall defined the edge of the chamber complex facing the outer court. This wall created the visual boundary between the public court and the priestly residential zone — separating where the people gathered from where the servants of the sanctuary lived.
42.8: Fifty cubits toward the outer court, a hundred cubits toward the temple — the priests' chambers extended deeper on the sacred side than on the public side. Proximity to the Most Holy shaped even the length of the service rooms.
42.9: A ground-level entry on the east side — the eastern approach maintained even to the service quarters. One entered the priests' chambers from the outer court, ascending to the inner levels from there: even the service workers moved through the court of assembly before entering their own domain.
42.10: The southern priests' chambers mirrored the northern ones, built into the court wall on the east side, facing the separate yard. The architecture maintained strict bilateral symmetry: north and south priests' chambers matched in position and proportion.
42.11: North and south chambers were identical — same corridor, same dimensions, same exits, same doors. The bilateral symmetry that governed the gates governed the service quarters as well. In God's house, order extends to every room, every passage, every arrangement.
42.12: A door at the head of the south passageway gave access from the east wall corridor. Every entry point was intentional, every passage assigned a direction. The priests could move through a network of corridors and chambers, all mapped and measured, without confusion or improvisation.
42.13: 'The priests who approach Yahweh' — the defining phrase for the Zadokite priesthood in Ezekiel (43:19; 44:15; 45:4). The holiest portions of every sacrifice — the portions not burned on the altar but eaten by the priests — were consumed here, in these chambers, in the presence of God. The meal of the holy things was itself an act of priestly worship: to eat what is holy is to participate in holiness.
42.14: The garment protocol: priestly vestments were holy — charged with the holiness of the sanctuary — and could not be worn into the common space of the outer court. The priests changed clothes before entering the people's space. The boundary between holy and common was maintained in fabric as well as in stone. Holiness is not ambient; it must be deliberately managed at every transition.
42.15: The interior survey is complete; the measuring angel exits through the east gate — the gate of arrival, the gate of God's returning glory. The final measurements will be the overall dimensions, the grand summary. The east gate frames both the beginning and the end of the survey.
42.16: Five hundred reeds on the east side — each reed six long cubits, making the enclosure roughly 1,550 meters on a side by the long-cubit calculation. This is not a temple but a city-scale sacred precinct. The vast scale of the final enclosure surpasses anything built in Israel's history and signals that this vision is eschatological: a temple to fit an age of full restoration.
42.17: North side: five hundred reeds, identical to the east. The measurement is consistent — a perfect square on a massive scale. Nothing in God's restored sanctuary is lopsided or approximate.
42.18: South side: five hundred reeds. Three sides measured, all matching. The symmetry of the eschatological temple is absolute — it does not favor any compass direction, any approach, any people. God's house is equally vast on every side.
42.19: West side: five hundred reeds. The measuring is complete. Every direction has yielded the same measurement. The enclosure is a perfect square of five hundred reeds — geometrically complete, theologically complete, eschatologically complete.
42.20: The final verse of the architectural survey gives its purpose in seven words: 'to make a separation between the holy and the common.' The Hebrew verb is בָּדַל — the same word used in Genesis 1 when God separated light from darkness, waters above from waters below. The perimeter wall of the temple is a creation act: the establishment of sacred order over undifferentiated common space. The temple vision ends where Genesis 1 begins — with God's decisive act of separation, the first movement of all creation.

46.1: The Lord Yahweh declares: The east gate of the inner court stays shut through the six working days. On the Sabbath it opens. On the new moon it opens.
46.2: The prince enters through the outer vestibule of the gate and takes his place at the gate's post. The priests bring his burnt offering and peace offerings. He bows in worship at the gate's threshold, then withdraws. The gate remains open until evening.
46.3: The whole congregation of the land gathers to worship before Yahweh at the entrance of that gate on every Sabbath and every new moon.
46.4: On the Sabbath the prince brings Yahweh a burnt offering of six flawless lambs and one flawless ram.
46.5: Alongside the ram: one ephah of grain. Alongside the lambs: whatever he can provide — with a hin of oil accompanying each ephah.
46.6: For the new moon: a young bull without defect, six lambs, and a ram — all flawless.
46.7: The accompanying grain offering: one ephah with the bull, one ephah with the ram, and with the lambs whatever he can give — a hin of oil for each ephah.
46.8: When the prince enters for his regular worship, he enters by the gate vestibule and goes out the same way he came in.
46.9: At the great appointed festivals when the people come before Yahweh, no one doubles back through the gate they entered. Those coming in from the north exit from the south; those coming in from the south exit from the north. The flow is always forward, never reversed — the people move through worship, not back and forth across it.
46.10: The prince enters and exits with the people — not before them, not apart from them. He is a worshipper among worshippers.
46.11: At every feast and every appointed gathering, the same grain-offering formula applies: one ephah per bull, one ephah per ram, whatever is possible with the lambs, and a hin of oil for each ephah.
46.12: If the prince brings a voluntary offering — a burnt offering or peace offerings given freely, not required — the east gate is opened for him. He offers it as on the Sabbath, then departs, and the gate closes behind him. Even spontaneous worship has its proper form here.
46.13: Every morning, without exception, a yearling lamb without defect shall be offered to Yahweh as a burnt offering. Daily. Morning by morning.
46.14: With the lamb each morning: a grain offering — one-sixth of an ephah of fine flour moistened with one-third of a hin of oil. These are permanent statutes, not subject to revision.
46.15: Lamb, grain, oil — morning by morning, without fail. This is the regular burnt offering that never ceases.
46.16: The Lord Yahweh declares: If the prince grants any of his sons a gift of land as an inheritance, it belongs to them permanently. Gifts to sons become true inheritance.
46.17: But if he gives a portion to one of his servants, that grant runs only until the year of release — then it reverts to the prince. The prince's territorial inheritance can pass permanently only to his sons. Servants receive temporary use, not permanent possession.
46.18: The prince must never seize what belongs to my people. He cannot push them off their land to make room for his sons. He gives his sons only from his own holding — not one of my people is to be driven from what is theirs.
46.19: Then he guided me through the entrance at the side of the gate, into the row of sacred chambers along the north reserved for the priests. At the far western end I noticed a separate area.
46.20: He said: This is the priests' own kitchen. Here they cook the guilt offerings, the sin offerings, and the grain offerings — inside, away from the outer court. The reason: these most holy foods must not be carried into the common area, or holiness would flash across the boundary to those not prepared for it.
46.21: He led me out into the outer court and walked me around all four corners. In every corner there was an additional enclosed court.
46.22: Each of the four corner courts was identical: forty cubits long, thirty wide, enclosed on all sides — uniform in every corner of the outer court.
46.23: Inside each of these four courts ran a continuous row of stone construction, with permanent fire-hearths built into its base — the infrastructure for cooking on a large scale.
46.24: He said: These are the people's kitchens — where the temple servants cook the offerings that the congregation brings. The sacred system provides for everything: a place for each thing, each thing in its proper place.

47.1: Then he led me back to the entrance of the temple — and there it was: water seeping out from beneath the threshold, flowing east, for the temple faces east. It was coming from under the south side of the threshold, beneath the south side of the altar.
47.2: He brought me out through the north gate and walked me around on the outside to the outer east gate. There, from the south side, water was trickling out.
47.3: The man walked east with his measuring line and measured off a thousand cubits. He led me through the water — it came to my ankles.
47.4: Another thousand cubits — knee-deep. Another thousand — up to my waist. The water was rising with each measurement.
47.5: One more thousand — and there was no wading through it at all. The water had risen into a full river, deep enough to swim in, impossible to ford. What began as a trickle beneath the temple threshold had become an uncrossable river.
47.6: He said to me: Son of man — do you see what has happened? Then he walked me back to the bank.
47.7: As I turned back, I saw the banks: covered with dense trees on both sides, stretching as far as I could see.
47.8: He said: This water flows east, down into the Arabah, and pours into the Dead Sea. And when it does, the salt water turns fresh.
47.9: Wherever this river reaches, everything it touches will come alive — swarms of living creatures, fish beyond counting. For the water freshens everything it flows through. Life follows the river wherever it goes.
47.10: Fishermen will line its banks all the way from En-gedi to En-eglaim, spreading their nets. The fish will be as varied and plentiful as those of the Mediterranean.
47.11: But the stagnant pools and marshes cut off from the flowing current will not be healed — they are left as salt. Not everything is swept into the renewal; some things remain as they are, for preservation and for judgment.
47.12: On both banks of the river every kind of fruit tree will grow. Their leaves will never wither — like the righteous of Psalm 1, whose leaf does not wither. Their fruit will never fail; a fresh crop every month, because the water flowing through them comes from the sanctuary of Yahweh. The fruit feeds; the leaves heal. This is Eden's vision made new: not a garden behind us but ahead of us, and its source is the presence of God himself. The details match what John sees in Revelation 22 — river, trees, fruit, healing leaves — because both prophets are looking at the same thing.
47.13: The Lord Yahweh declares: These are the borders by which the land will be divided as an inheritance among the twelve tribes of Israel — with Joseph receiving a double share through his two sons.
47.14: Divide it equally — every tribe the same — because I swore an oath to your fathers, raising my hand to them, promising this land. Now the oath is fulfilled. The land falls to you as your inheritance.
47.15: The northern border of the land: from the Great Sea, along the road toward Hethlon, on to the entrance of Hamath, continuing to Zedad,
47.16: then Berothah, Sibraim — on the border between Damascus and Hamath — as far as Hazer-hatticon, bordering Hauran.
47.17: The northern line runs from the sea to Hazar-enan, along the northern edge of Damascus, with Hamath's border as its furthest reach. That is the north.
47.18: For the eastern boundary: the Jordan River runs between Hauran and Damascus, between Gilead and Israel's land, and continues down to the eastern sea. That is the east.
47.19: The southern border runs from Tamar to the waters of Meribah-kadesh — the place of Israel's ancient testing — then along the Brook of Egypt all the way to the Mediterranean. That is the south.
47.20: The western boundary is the Great Sea itself, running from the south all the way up to the point directly opposite the entrance of Hamath. That is the west.
47.21: This is the land. Divide it among the tribes of Israel.
47.22: And here is something unprecedented: the foreigners who have settled among you and raised their children here are to receive a share of the land alongside you. They are to be treated as native-born Israelites — full tribal allotments, the same as any son of the covenant. This goes beyond anything in the Pentateuchal legislation; it is the eschatological vision of a community defined not by bloodline but by residence in the place where Yahweh dwells.
47.23: Wherever a resident alien has made his home, that is the tribe that gives him his allotment. This is the word of the Lord Yahweh.

48.1: Here are the tribal allotments, laid out in strips from the northern edge to the south. At the furthest northern boundary — from the road of Hethlon toward Lebo-hamath as far as Hazar-enan, along Damascus's northern edge, facing Hamath — running east to west: Dan's portion.
48.2: Directly south of Dan, east to west: Asher's portion.
48.3: South of Asher, east to west: Naphtali's portion.
48.4: South of Naphtali, east to west: Manasseh's portion.
48.5: South of Manasseh, east to west: Ephraim's portion — the other half of Joseph's double share.
48.6: South of Ephraim, east to west: Reuben's portion.
48.7: South of Reuben, east to west: Judah's portion — the tribe of the Davidic line, placed immediately north of the sacred center.
48.8: South of Judah, east to west, lies the sacred district — the terumah set apart for Yahweh. It matches the tribal strips in width: twenty-five thousand cubits across. At its center stands the sanctuary.
48.9: The holy portion for Yahweh: twenty-five thousand cubits long, twenty thousand wide.
48.10: Within the holy district, the priests' allotment is a strip twenty-five thousand cubits long and ten thousand cubits wide. The sanctuary of Yahweh stands at its center.
48.11: This land belongs to the consecrated priests — the sons of Zadok, who held their post and did not follow Israel into idolatry when the Levites did. Faithfulness during the worst hour of Israel's history earned them this honor.
48.12: This is their consecrated share — the most holy section, set right next to the Levites' land.
48.13: The Levites' strip lies alongside the priests': twenty-five thousand cubits long, ten thousand wide — equal in size, neighboring in position.
48.14: This land cannot be sold, traded, or transferred in any way. The first and best portion of the land belongs to Yahweh — it is permanently outside the market.
48.15: The remaining strip — five thousand cubits wide by twenty-five thousand long — serves a common purpose: the city itself, its residential areas, and surrounding open land. The city sits at the center of this strip.
48.16: The city itself is a perfect square: four thousand five hundred cubits on each of the four sides.
48.17: Around the city on every side runs a two-hundred-and-fifty-cubit buffer of open land — a suburban margin separating the city walls from the surrounding fields.
48.18: On either side of the city block — ten thousand cubits east, ten thousand cubits west — runs farmland. Its harvest provides food for those who work in the city.
48.19: The people who work in the city will come from all twelve tribes of Israel. This is not Levi's city or Judah's city — it belongs to all the people.
48.20: The complete sacred district is a square: twenty-five thousand cubits on each side. Within it, the holy portion and the city's land are held together as one unified allocation.
48.21: Everything that flanks the sacred district on both sides — stretching east to the land's eastern border, west to its western border, matching the depth of the tribal strips — belongs to the prince. At the center of it all: the holy district and the sanctuary of the temple.
48.22: Within the prince's flanking territory, the land lying between Judah's border on the north and Benjamin's border on the south — cutting through the Levites' zone and the city zone — also belongs to the prince.
48.23: Now the southern tribes, mirroring the northern arrangement. First, directly south of the sacred district, east to west: Benjamin's portion.
48.24: South of Benjamin, east to west: Simeon's portion.
48.25: South of Simeon, east to west: Issachar's portion.
48.26: South of Issachar, east to west: Zebulun's portion.
48.27: South of Zebulun, east to west: Gad's portion — the southernmost tribe's allotment.
48.28: Gad's southern border is also the land's southern border: from Tamar to the waters of Meribah-kadesh, then along the Brook of Egypt to the Great Sea — the same line drawn in 47:19.
48.29: That is the land. Allotted, divided, given to each tribe of Israel as their own. This is the word of the Lord Yahweh.
48.30: And now — the city's gates. On the north wall, four thousand five hundred cubits wide:
48.31: three gates, named after the tribes of Israel: the gate of Reuben, the gate of Judah, the gate of Levi.
48.32: On the east wall, four thousand five hundred cubits: three gates — Joseph, Benjamin, Dan.
48.33: On the south wall, four thousand five hundred cubits: three gates — Simeon, Issachar, Zebulun.
48.34: On the west wall, four thousand five hundred cubits: three gates — Gad, Asher, Naphtali. Twelve gates, twelve tribes. John sees the same gates in Revelation 21:12.
48.35: The full circuit of the city walls: eighteen thousand cubits. And the name of the city — from that day forward, forever — shall be: Yahweh Is There. This is the final word of the book of Ezekiel. The glory that lifted from the cherubim and abandoned the temple (chs. 10–11), that hovered east of the city before departing, has returned (43:1–5) and will never leave again. The city is no longer described by its guilt or its gates or its dimensions. It is named for its one defining reality: God is there.
